questions.optionB: Destruction of superficial layers of skin (epidermis) by friction or scraping against a rough surface
questions.explanation: Abrasion: superficial injury involving only the epidermis (skin surface). Types: scratches (linear), grazes (sliding), pressure abrasions (patterned — imprint of object, e.g., ligature mark, bite mark, tire tread), impact abrasion. Forensic significance: indicates point of impact, direction of force, nature of weapon/surface, patterned abrasions reveal weapon shape. Healing: no scar (epidermis regenerates completely). Ante-mortem abrasion: vital reaction (reddish-brown). Post-mortem: yellow, translucent, parchment-like. Reference: Reddy — Abrasions.

questions.questionText: The difference between an incised wound and a laceration is:
questions.optionB: Incised wound: sharp/cutting weapon (clean edges, no bridging), Laceration: blunt force (irregular edges, tissue bridging, bruising)
questions.explanation: Incised wound (cut): caused by sharp-edged weapon (knife, razor, glass). Features: clean-cut margins, no tissue bridging, no bruising, bleeds freely, length > depth, tailing at ends. Laceration (tear): caused by blunt force. Features: irregular/ragged margins, TISSUE BRIDGES (intact blood vessels/nerves crossing wound — most important differentiating feature), bruised margins, less bleeding (vessels crushed), hair follicles intact. Stab wound: incised wound where depth > length (sharp pointed weapon). Reference: Reddy — Wounds.
questions.conceptTag: Incised wound vs laceration tissue bridging

questions.questionText: Hesitation cuts (tentative wounds) are typically seen in:
questions.optionB: Suicide (multiple superficial, parallel cuts near the fatal wound — commonly on wrists, neck)
questions.explanation: Hesitation cuts: multiple superficial, parallel incised wounds near the fatal wound site. Seen in SUICIDE — person hesitates before inflicting the final deep wound. Common sites: wrist (most common), neck, antecubital fossa. Indicate self-inflicted injury. Defense wounds: seen in HOMICIDE — victim's palms, fingers, forearms (trying to ward off attacker). Fabricated wounds: self-inflicted to simulate assault (superficial, accessible areas, clothing intact). Reference: Reddy — Suicidal Wounds.

questions.questionText: The age of a bruise (contusion) can be estimated by its color change:
questions.optionB: Red/blue (fresh) → blue/purple (1-3 days) → green (4-7 days) → yellow (7-14 days) → normal (2-4 weeks)
questions.explanation: Bruise color changes reflect hemoglobin degradation: Fresh (0-2 days): red/blue (deoxyhemoglobin). 1-3 days: blue-purple. 4-7 days: green (biliverdin). 7-14 days: yellow (bilirubin). 2-4 weeks: normal skin. Limitations: unreliable in dark skin, overlapping colors, observer variability, site and depth variation. Contusion: extravasation of blood in subcutaneous tissues due to blunt force. Deep bruises may appear later and laterally (tracking of blood). Artificial bruise: painted (no swelling, no tenderness). Reference: Reddy — Bruise Dating.

questions.questionText: Entry wound of a firearm differs from exit wound in that entry wound:
questions.optionB: Has a collar of abrasion (abrasion collar/marginal abrasion), inverted margins, blackening/tattooing (contact/close range), and is usually smaller than exit
questions.explanation: Entry wound features: (1) Abrasion collar (marginal abrasion/grease collar — bullet scrapes skin), (2) Inverted margins (punched-in), (3) Usually small and round/oval, (4) Blackening (soot — contact/close range), (5) Tattooing (unburnt powder grains — close range), (6) Singeing of hair (contact). Exit wound: irregular/stellate, larger, EVERTED margins, no abrasion collar, no blackening/tattooing. Contact wound: muzzle impression, soot inside wound, stellate tear (skull — gases expand). Reference: Reddy — Firearm Injuries.
